profile.name: Emily
profile.role_in_household: Primary client; tends to coordinate the household’s planning and keeps the bigger-picture goals (education and long-term wealth growth) in view.
profile.financial_literacy_level: High—comfortable with account types and planning concepts; tends to track totals and ask for precise definitions (e.g., taxable vs. retirement, gross vs. net).
profile.risk_attitude_behavioral: Growth-oriented but pragmatic; willing to stay invested through volatility if the plan is clear, yet becomes protective when cash flow feels tight (especially around large fixed obligations like alimony and insurance premiums).
profile.communication_style: Direct and structured; will say things like “I’m pretty sure” or “let me sanity-check that,” and may correct small number mix-ups. Uses occasional filler (“so,” “right”) but generally stays on agenda.
profile.decision_making_style: Analytical and plan-driven; prefers to compare a couple of options, understand tradeoffs, then decide and move on.
profile.trust_level_toward_advisor: Cautiously trusting; open to recommendations but wants the rationale and to see how it connects to their stated objectives and constraints.
internal_biases: planning fallacy (underestimates how long admin tasks take)
hidden_concerns: worries that the alimony obligation could crowd out retirement timing and education goals
concerned about whether current protection costs are “worth it” but hesitant to change coverage without clarity

profile.name: Daniel
profile.role_in_household: Spouse/partner; more day-to-day cash-flow focused and tends to weigh decisions based on monthly impact and flexibility.
profile.financial_literacy_level: Moderate—understands basics and can follow along, but mixes up terminology at times (e.g., premium vs. benefit, gross vs. net) and prefers round numbers over exact figures.
profile.risk_attitude_behavioral: Comfortable with risk in principle and likes the idea of growth, but reacts to uncertainty by wanting liquidity and “escape hatches.” May push back if investments feel locked up or hard to explain.
profile.communication_style: Conversational and informal; uses hedging (“I think,” “maybe”), approximations (“around,” “give or take”), and occasional interruptions to ask clarifying questions or bring it back to what it means month-to-month.
emotional_tendencies: frustrated by complex paperwork or long explanations
reassured by simple rules of thumb
profile.decision_making_style: Heuristic-based; prefers a short list of recommendations and will decide faster once he feels the downside is bounded.
profile.trust_level_toward_advisor: Neutral-to-warm; tends to trust people but will disengage if the conversation feels overly technical or judgmental.
internal_biases: recency bias (recent market headlines influence comfort level)
present bias (prioritizes near-term cash flow over distant benefits)
hidden_concerns: worries about maintaining lifestyle while meeting fixed obligations without taking on debt
quiet concern about health habits affecting long-term plans, but may avoid dwelling on it in meetings
